coredata.dc:title: Development of stimuli-responsive lyotropic liquid crystalline nanoparticles targeting lysosomes: Physicochemical, morphological and drug release studies
coredata.dc:creator: Chountoulesi, Maria
Perinelli, Diego Romano

Demetzos, Costas
coredata.dc:description: The abilities of sub-cellular targeting and stimuli-responsiveness are critical challenges in pharmaceutical nanotechnology. In the present study, glyceryl monooleate (GMO)-based non-lamellar lyotropic liquid crystalline nanoparticles were stabilized by the poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate)-b-poly(lauryl methacrylate) block copolymer carrying tri-phenyl-phosphine cations (TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA), either used alone or in combination with other polymers as co-stabilizers. The systems were designed to perform simultaneously sub-cellular targeting, stimuli-responsiveness and to exhibit stealthiness. The physicochemical characteristics and fractal dimensions of the resultant nanosystems were obtained from light scattering techniques, while their micropolarity and microfluidity from fluorescence spectroscopy. Their morphology was assessed by cryo-TEM, while their thermal behavior by microcalorimetry and high-resolution ultrasound spectroscopy. The analyzed properties, including the responsiveness to pH and temperature, were found to be dependent on the combination of the polymeric stabilizers. The subcellular localization was monitored by confocal microscopy, revealing targeting to lysosomes. Subsequently, resveratrol was loaded into the nanosystems, the entrapment efficiency was investigated and in vitro release studies were carried out at different conditions, in which a stimuli-triggered drug release profile was achieved. In conclusion, the proposed multi-functional nanosystems can be considered as potentially stealth, stimuli-responsive drug delivery nanocarriers, with targeting ability to lysosomes and presenting a stimuli-triggered drug release profile.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abilities of sub-cellular targeting and stimuli-responsiveness are critical challenges in pharmaceutical nanotechnology. In the present study, glyceryl monooleate (GMO)-based non-lamellar lyotropic liquid crystalline nanoparticles were stabilized by the poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate)-b-poly(lauryl methacrylate) block copolymer carrying tri-phenyl-phosphine cations (TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA), either used alone or in combination with other polymers as co-stabilizers. The systems were designed to perform simultaneously sub-cellular targeting, stimuli-responsiveness and to exhibit stealthiness. The physicochemical characteristics and fractal dimensions of the resultant nanosystems were obtained from light scattering techniques, while their micropolarity and microfluidity from fluorescence spectroscopy. Their morphology was assessed by cryo-TEM, while their thermal behavior by microcalorimetry and high-resolution ultrasound spectroscopy. The analyzed properties, including the responsiveness to pH and temperature, were found to be dependent on the combination of the polymeric stabilizers. The subcellular localization was monitored by confocal microscopy, revealing targeting to lysosomes. Subsequently, resveratrol was loaded into the nanosystems, the entrapment efficiency was investigated and in vitro release studies were carried out at different conditions, in which a stimuli-triggered drug release profile was achieved. In conclusion, the proposed multi-functional nanosystems can be considered as potentially stealth, stimuli-responsive drug delivery nanocarriers, with targeting ability to lysosomes and presenting a stimuli-triggered drug release profile.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lyotropic liquid crystalline nanoparticles exhibit highly ordered 2-or 3-dimentional nanostructures of hydrophilic aqueous channels being embedded in a lipid matrix, forming cubic (i.d. cubosomes) or hexagonal (i.d. hexosomes) mesophases and are considered as promising delivery nanosystems of pharmaceutically active compounds (Zhai et al. 2019; Barriga et al. 2019; Chountoulesi et al. 2022; Oliveira et al. 2022). These nanosystems require the use of a steric stabilizer, in order to get dispersed in aqueous media, most usually an amphiphilic polymer as the triblock copolymer Poloxamer 407 (P407). Some of their main advantages are the larger internal lipid bilayer-water interfacial surface, enabling high drug entrapment efficiency and the possibility of controlled drug release (Azmi et al., 2015; Chountoulesi et al. 2018; Umar et al. 2022).

Since the targets of many therapeutic agents against several disorders are localized in subcellular compartments, such as mitochondria, nucleus, lysosomes and endoplasmic reticulum, the development of nanomedicines for precise delivery to subcellular compartments remains a formidable challenge. From a biopharmaceutical point of view, precise drug delivery should be targeted not only at cell level, but also at organelle level to achieve the maximum therapeutic responses, to facilitate therapeutic dosing, as well as to avoid possible toxic side effects of the drugs. Moreover, the subcellular drug delivery will overcome critical limitations of drug actions, such as multidrug resistance, which has been a major hurdle in therapies, namely in cancer chemotherapy. The precise and selective delivery of therapeutic agents to intracellular targets includes many steps, starting from an efficient cellular uptake, to the protection of the nanocarrier inside the cytosol, and consequently the selective uptake by the targeted subcellular organelle. Thus, the design of novel nanoparticles has been employing smart materials such as polymers (Rajendran et al. 2010; Huang et al. 2011; Ma et al. 2016; He et al., 2021; Guo et al. 2020; Papadimitriou et al. 2022).

Lysosomes are considered to be a therapeutically important intracellular organelle as targets for nanomedicine-based treatment of various lysosomal dysfunction related diseases, such as lysosomal storage disorders, cancer, inflammatory and neurodegenerative disorders, including Alzheimer’s Disease and autoimmune diseases. For instance, the development of lysosome targeted anticancer drug delivery systems is based on the increased uptake of the carriers by the lysosomal compartment that determines the subsequent destruction of lysosomes and killing of cancer cells (Del Grosso et al. 2022; Lee et al. 2011a; Platt et al. 2012; Muro et al. 2006; Giannotti et al., 2011; Bonam et al. 2019; Wang et al. 2018; Pinto e Vairo et al. 2020). Yin et al. (2022) used nanoparticles as a tool to target the cellular autophagy–lysosome system for cell modulation, opening a new avenue for the therapeutic rescue of inflammation-compromised stem cells. Moreover, nanoparticles being able to localize at the lysosome, are useful towards real-time monitoring and imaging into the cell (Zou et al. 2022; Barbosa et al. 2022).

The aim of the present study is the development of new lyotropic glyceryl monooleate (GMO)–based liquid crystalline nanoparticles, with multi-functional properties, namely targeting ability to lysosomes, dual stimuli-responsiveness (pH- and thermo-) permitting a triggered drug release profile. In this study, the advantages of the liquid crystalline materials in the formulation of advanced drug delivery nanosystems are being upgraded with the targeting ability towards lysosomes. Resveratrol, being an active pharmaceutical agent that gets involved to many cellular paths correlated also with lysosomal dysfunctions (Trincheri et al. 2007), was chosen to be encapsulated in the systems. For this purpose, we incorporated as polymeric stabilizer an amphiphilic block copolymer (TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA), consisted of the hydrophobic poly(lauryl methacrylate) (PLMA) block and the hydrophilic stimuli-responsive PDMAEMA block that was partially quaternized by the delocalized, tri-phenyl-phosphine (TPP) cation, in order to have a high cationic content for efficient cellular internalization. The above polymer was used either alone or in combination with other stabilizers, namely Poloxamer P407 (P407) and poly(ethylene oxide)-block-poly(ε-caprolactone) (PEO-b-PCL) block copolymers, as well as the poly(2-methyl-2-oxazoline)-grad-poly(2-phenyl-2-oxazoline) (MPOx) gradient copolymer that all exhibit high biocompatibility, low toxicity, as well as promising stealth properties.

The systems were evaluated by using a gamut of light scattering techniques (dynamic, static and electrophoretic) for the physicochemical characterisation. Microcalorimetry and high-resolution ultrasonic spectroscopy were used for the investigation of their thermal behaviour. Cryo-TEM was utilized for the morphological evaluation of the initial state of the nanoparticles. Moreover, their microenvironmental parameters, were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy. Subsequently, the hydrophobic drug molecule resveratrol was incorporated and in vitro release studies were carried out. Finally, the in vitro toxicity of the nanosystems and their subcellular localization to lysosomes was monitored.

To the best of our knowledge this is the first report on non-lamellar, liquid crystalline, drug delivery nanoparticles, employing the aforementioned combination of polymeric stabilizers. Moreover, for the first time, liquid crystalline nanoparticles were designed to perform simultaneously stimuli-responsiveness for triggered drug release, as well as subcellular targeting to lysosomes.

All liquid crystalline nanosystems were prepared from glyceryl monooleate lipid Monomuls® 90-O18 [GMO, 1-(cis-9-octadecenoyl)-rac-glycerol], which was purchased from BASF (Ludwigshafen, Germany) and used without further purification. The TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA, PEO-b-PCL and MPOx copolymers were synthesized as described in paragraph 2.2.1. Pluronic® F-127 (Poloxamer P407) (PEO98-PPO67-PEO98), with an average molecular weight of 12,600 g/mol, as well as resveratrol, were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co (St. Louis, MO, USA). All formulations were prepared in HPLC-grade water. Chloroform, ethanol and acetone were of analytical grade and purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Co. Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was purchased from Gibco®. Pyrene was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Co. and dissolved at appropriate concentration (1 mM) in acetone. Sodium chloride (NaCl) salt, citric acid, citrate sodium and Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) tablets were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Chemical Co. and dissolved in HPLC-grade water at appropriate concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and chemical characterization of the block and gradient copolymers
ce:section.ce:para: Reversible addition fragmentation chain transfer polymerization (RAFT) was employed to synthesize the PDMAEMA-b-PLMA amphiphilic block copolymer, following a two-step polymerization procedure, as described in details previously (Chrysostomou and Pispas, 2018). The tertiary amine group of the PDMAEMA block of the PDMAEMA-b-PLMA copolymer was partially modified to quaternary ammonium salt, by utilizing 4-bromobutyl triphenyl phosphonium bromide (Sigma-Aldrich) as the quaternization agent. The quaternization reaction is described in detail in SI. The quaternization degree was assessed by Proton Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy and was found 20 %. The synthesis of poly(ethylene oxide)-block-poly(ε-caprolactone) (PEO-b-PCL) amphiphilic block copolymers is described in details by Pippa et al. 2013a; Chountoulesi et al. 2021. Two different PEO-b-PCL copolymers were synthesized, named H1 and H4, with a different content %wt of hydrophobic PCL, 15 % and 30 % respectively. The synthesis of poly(2-methyl-2-oxazoline)-grad-poly(2-phenyl-2-oxazoline) (MPOx) amphiphilic gradient (or pseudo-diblock) copolymers is described in detail by Milonaki et al. 2012; Chountoulesi et al. 2021. Two different copolymers were synthesized, namely MPOx1 and MPOx2, containing different content of the hydrophobic segment (PhOx), 32 % and 12 % respectively. The synthesized copolymers were characterized by size exclusion chromatography (SEC) and 1H NMR. Their molecular characteristics are summarized in Table S1, and their chemical structures are illustrated in Figure S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of liquid crystalline nanoparticle dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: Two different weight ratios were prepared, namely GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 9:1 and 4:1 respectively, which correspond to two different polymeric concentrations, namely 10 % and 20 % w/w relative to the lipid mass. Subsequently, the TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA stabilizer was combined with PEO-b-PCL H1, PEO-b-PCL H4, MPOx1, MPOx2, as well as with P407 in 1:1 wt ratio, providing a total stabilizer concentration of 20 % w/w relative to the lipid mass (GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA: PEO-b-PCL H1/PEO-b-PCL H4/MPOx1/MPOx2:Poloxamer P407 8:1:1 wt % ratio). The lipid concentration was 20 mg/mL in all prepared systems. The temperature for the preparation process was 45 °C and was chosen according to the temperature-concentration phase diagram of GMO in water, being also the minimum temperature that could be used without overtaking the LCST of the PDMAEMA block (Chountoulesi et al. 2019).

All liquid crystalline systems were prepared by Top-Down Method (TD). More analytically, GMO was weighted into glass vials and heated to 45 °C, until free flowing. The appropriate volume of HPLC-grade water solution (pH = 6.0), containing the different amounts of the polymeric stabilizers, was added to the vials containing the lipids, in order to achieve a lipid concentration of 20 mg/mL. The mixtures were firstly sonicated using a bath sonicator for 2 min, at 45 °C, followed by two 2-min sonication cycles (amplitude 70, cycle 0.7), interrupted by a 2-min resting period, using a probe sonicator (UP 200 S, DrHielsher GmbH, Berlin, Germany), until a milky dispersion was formed. The resultant dispersions were allowed to anneal for 30 min, and then stored at room temperature. In the cases of liquid crystalline systems with entrapped resveratrol, the appropriate amounts of resveratrol and GMO were fully dissolved in ethanol. The ethanol was gently evaporated until a dry film of lipid-resveratrol mixture was achieved. The mixture was heated to 45 °C, until free flowing and the same process described above for the empty liquid crystalline nanosystems was repeated. The concentration of the resveratrol in the final dispersion was 2 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical and morphological characterization of the liquid crystalline dispersions
Dynamic, static and electrophoretic light scattering techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The physicochemical behavior of the prepared nanosystems was characterized by measuring the size (hydrodynamic radius Rh , nm) and the size distribution (polydispersity index, PDI) by utilizing dynamic light scattering (DLS), while the ζ-potential (ζ-pot, mV) of the nanoparticles was measured by electrophoretic light scattering (ELS). 100 μL of aliquots were 30-fold diluted in HPLC grade water. The protocols used for DLS, ELS and SLS techniques are described in detail in our previous studies (Chountoulesi et al. 2018; Pippa et al. 2012; Pippa et al., 2013a). The Rh , PDI and ζ-pot values of the nanoparticles were averaged from triplicate measurements and the results were reported as a mean ± standard deviation.

The effect of pH in three different media (HPLC-grade water with pH = 6.0, Phosphate Buffered Saline, PBS with pH = 7.4 and Citrate Buffer with pH = 4.2), serum proteins (Fetal Bovine Serum, FBS), ionic strength (NaCl 0.10 N, 0.34 N, 0.51 N and PBS 0.154 N) and temperature (25 °C, 37 °C and 55 °C) on the physicochemical behavior of the nanosystems was monitored by using DLS, SLS and ELS measurements, as described in our previous studies (Chountoulesi et al. 2018; Chountoulesi et al. 2019). The effect of temperature and pH on Rg/Rh and df was also studied, as described in Pippa et al. (2012), Pippa et al. (2013a), Chountoulesi et al. (2020).

Cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (Cryo-TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Tecnai F20 X TWIN microscope (FEI Company, USA) equipped with field emission gun, operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Images were recorded on the Eagle 4 k HS camera (FEI Company, USA) and processed with TIA software (FEI Company, USA). Specimen preparation was done by vitrification of the aqueous (HPLC-grade water) solutions on grids with holey carbon film (Quantifoil R 2/2; Quantifoil Micro Tools GmbH, Germany). Prior to use, the grids were activated for 15 s in oxygen plasma using a Femto plasma cleaner (Diener Electronic, Germany). Cryo-samples were prepared by applying a droplet (3 μL) of the solution to the grid, blotting with filter paper and immediate freezing in liquid ethane using a fully automated blotting device Vitrobot Mark IV (FEI Company, USA). After preparation, the vitrified specimens were kept under liquid nitrogen until they were inserted into a cryo-TEM holder Gatan 626 (Gatan Inc., USA) and analyzed in the TEM at −178 °C.

Cryo-TEM measurements were carried out on the initially prepared dispersions, both the empty ones and the ones with the incorporated drug, as well as on the empty dispersions after having been placed in conditions simulating the pathological ones. More specifically, the pH of the dispersions was decreased in 4.2 (by adding drop-wise hydrochloric acid 1 N) and subsequently they were incubated at 42 °C for 30 min. Then, the cryo-TEM measurements were repeated as described above.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was applied in order to extract some qualitative information about the internal microenvironment of the prepared nanoparticles (micropolarity and microfluidity), by using pyrene as hydrophobic probe. The intensity ratio of peak 1 to peak 3 of the pyrene (I1/I3 ), serves as a measure of the micropolarity, i.e. a larger I1/I3 ratio designates a higher polarity of the medium surrounding the probe. The microfluidity was estimated from the fluorescence intensity ratio IE/IM , where IE and IM are the fluorescence intensities of pyrene excimer and monomer, being measured at emission wavelengths of 480 nm and 372 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio IE/IM serves as an index of microfluidity, because the ratio IE/IM is proportional to the frequency of collisions of pyrene molecules, namely the ability to form excimers. Typically, larger values of IE/IM imply a larger tendency for excimer formation which can be indicative of a larger microfluidity in the internal domains of the nanosystem. The measurements were carried out at two different temperatures 25 °C and 45 °C, as well as at two different pH values 6.0 and 4.2. The protocol is described in detail by Chountoulesi et al. (2018), Chountoulesi et al. (2019), Pippa et al. (2012), Pippa et al. (2013a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of thermal behavior of the liquid crystalline dispersions

ce:section.ce:para: Calorimetric analyses were performed using a microDSC III (Setaram, France). Thermal scans were recorded in the temperature range from 5 °C to 80 °C at 1 °C/min rate. Temperature (Tm, °C) and enthalpy associated to the transition (ΔH, J/g of solution) were calculated from the peak and the area of the transition using the tangent method. All measurements were performed in triplicate (Chountoulesi et al. 2021).

Differential relative ultrasonic velocity for samples as a function of temperature were collected using a HR-US 102 high-resolution spectrometer (Ultrasonic Scientific, Ireland) by applying on the sample the same thermal programme of mDSC analyses (from 5 °C to 80 °C at 1 °C/min). All measurements were performed in triplicate (Chountoulesi et al. 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the liquid crystalline dispersions with entrapped resveratrol

ce:section.ce:para:
The free resveratrol was separated from the resveratrol entrapped in the liquid crystalline nanoparticles using the ultrafiltration centrifugal method (Mansour et al. 2017; Li et al. 2019; Chountoulesi et al. 2021). In details, the nanoparticle dispersion was centrifuged for 45 min at 8000 rpm using centrifugal filter tubes (molecular weight (MW) cutoff = 10 kDa; Millipore) at 4 °C. The particles were separated from the aqueous phase, and the free resveratrol was analyzed in the supernatant. The UV − Vis spectrophotometry method was used to measure the free drug concentration in the samples after centrifugation and supernatant collection. The absorption measurements were performed at a wavelength of 307 nm, by using UV − Vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu PharmaSpec UV-1700 UV/Vis spectrophotometer) and a preconstructed calibration curve. Centrifuged samples of the respective empty liquid crystalline nanoparticles were used as a blank. The Entrapment Efficiency (EE)% was calculated according to the following equation
E E

C
supernatant
C

%
( 1 )
where Csupernatant is the resveratrol concentration that was quantified in the supernatant (not entrapped) and Ctotal is the total concentration of the resveratrol added in the dispersion (2 mg/mL).

The in vitro release of resveratrol from the freshly prepared liquid crystalline nanoparticles was assessed in the dark, by using the dialysis method (Mansour et al. 2017; Li et al. 2019; Pippa et al. 2013b; Pippa et al. 2014; Kulkarni et al. 2017; Chountoulesi et al. 2021). A specific volume of the dispersions (0.5 mL) were placed into dialysis sacks (molecular weight cut off 12,000; Sigma-Aldrich, flat width 10 mm and diameter 6.5 mm), as described by Chountoulesi et al. 2021. The in vitro release study was carried out in two different experimental conditions. The dialysis sacks were inserted in 20 mL PBS (pH = 7.4), in shaking water bath set at 37 °C (simulating physiological conditions), as well as in 20 mL Citrate Buffer (pH = 4.2), in shaking water bath set at 42 °C (simulating pathological conditions). Aliquots of samples (0.5 mL) were taken from the external solution at specific time intervals and that volume was replaced with fresh release medium. Samples were diluted with ethanol and quantified for drug content by UV–vis spectrophotometric analysis at 307 nm (Shimadzu PharmaSpec UV-1700 UV/Vis spectrophotometer). Empty liquid crystalline nanoparticles were used as a blank. The cumulative % drug mass released versus time was plotted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro studies in cell lines

ce:section.ce:para: African green monkey kidney fibroblast-like cell line (COS-7) cells were plated (10 × 103) into 96-well plates in RPMI medium. The next day the cells were treated with the selected liquid crystalline nanoparticles, in 1, 5, 10 and 50 μΜ concentrations, for 24 h. Following treatment, the mitochondrial redox function, translated as cell viability, was assessed by the MTT assay (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MA, USA). The cell medium in each well was replaced with MTT solution (1 mg/mL in RPMI) and cells were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. MTT was then removed from all cells and the produced formazan crystals were solubilized with 2-propanol. The plates were subsequently shaken briefly and the absorbance was measured at 540 nm using an Infinite M200 microplate reader (Tecan group ltd., Männedorf, Switzerland). Blank values measured in wells with 2-propanol without cells, were in all cases subtracted. The relative cell viability was determined as cell survival percentage compared to untreated cells which were used as control. Statistical significance for the various treatments was assessed with the Student’s t-test using GraphPad Prism software (GraphPad Inc.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) experimental set up
ce:section.ce:para: COS-7 cells were seeded on μ–Dish35 mm dishes (ibidi GmbH, Martinsried, Germany) and left to grow overnight in 2 mL of RPMI medium with 10 % FBS under the same conditions as elaborated above in the cell culture section. Cells were subsequently incubated with the selected liquid crystalline nanoparticles for 3 h in RPMI medium. The liquid crystalline nanoparticles, which were administrated in cell culture for the CLSM studies, had been previously labelled with rhodamine-conjugated to phospholipid, namely with the 1,2-dimyristoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-(lissamine rhodamine B sulfonyl) (ammonium salt, Rh-PE), acquired by Avanti Polar Lipids Inc. (Alabaster, AL). LysoTrackerTM DND-26 (ThermoFisher) was added at a final concentration of 50 nM. CLSM studies were performed on an inverted Leica-TCS SP8 confocal microscope (Wetzlar, Germany), with an oil-immersion 63x objective magnification lens. A 488 nm (for LysoTracker) and a 561 nm for (Rhodamine) solid state laser, with high sensitivity-spectral detectors were used. Images were acquired with the LAS X software (Leica Microsystems CMS GmbH). Colocalization was quantitated using Pearson's correlation coefficient. Imaging data from the experiments were analyzed using the Coloc 2 plugin of the Fiji distribution of ImageJ software (https://imagej.net/Coloc_2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The physicochemical characteristics of the prepared nanosystems obtained by light scattering methods
ce:section.ce:para: The TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA copolymer was found to provide GMO-based colloidal dispersions, either by being used individually or in combination with the other polymers as co-stabilizers. According to Table S2, the use of TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA copolymer as single stabilizer or in association with the other polymeric co-stabilizers did not strongly affect the physicochemical characteristics of the obtained nanosystems, which presented similar sizes (hydrodynamic radius) and size distributions (PDI values). Moreover, all the prepared nanosystems were found to present high positive ζ-potential values, probably due to the high polycationic content of the TPP groups, as well as due to the free amino groups of the un-quaternized PDMAEMA blocks that are partially positively ionized in HPCL water with pH = 6.0. According to our previous studies, the P407, PEO-b-PCL and MPOx copolymers being individually used as stabilizers (Chountoulesi et al. 2018; Chountoulesi et al. 2021) result in negative ζ-potential. Thus, we can argue that the TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA copolymer provides successfully a high positive surface charge, overcoming the effect of the other stabilizers. Regarding the macroscopic examination, all the dispersions exhibited milky opaque appearance, which is an indication of a well-defined liquid crystalline structure, due to a strong Rayleigh scattering to visible light (Akhlaghi et al. 2016; Liu et al. 2013).

Colloidal stability over time was also investigated, by measuring the size and size distribution of the nanosystems for a period of 40 days. The majority of systems were stable upon time, exhibiting no significant change of both size and size distribution (Figures S2, S3). The less stable system was the one with PEO-b-PCL H4 as second stabilizer that exhibited the largest size alteration after the 18th day from the preparation, when the larger particles were aggregated and adhered on the vial, while the smaller ones remained dispersed and stable over time. We may assume that the increase of hydrophobic PCL content reduced the physicochemical stability in a time-dependent manner, because the higher hydrophilic-to-hydrophobic ratio of the polymer, the more efficient stabilization is created, due to a greater entropic effect (Chong et al. 2011; 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the dilution medium on the physicochemical behavior of the nanosystems
ce:section.ce:para: In our previous investigation (Chountoulesi et al. 2019), we incorporated for the first time as stabilizer a polycation of PDMAEMA and more specifically the PDMAEMA-b-PLMA block copolymer, yielding liquid crystalline nanoparticles with pH- and temperature responsiveness. In the present study, we tried to combine both the stimuli-responsiveness property and the high cationic potential for cellular internalization, applied for the first time in liquid crystalline formulations. Thus, we used the TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA stabilizer, which is the PDMAEMA-b-PLMA block copolymer, partially quaternized with TPP cation in the PDMAEMA part, possessing in parallel free tertiary amine groups in the PDMAEMA block. Apart from HPLC grade water (pH = 6.0), which is the preparation medium of all the dispersions, PBS buffer with pH = 7.4 was used to verify whether the nanosystems would be stable during their potential administration in physiological blood conditions, while Citrate buffer with pH = 4.2 was used to investigate the response of the nanosystems to acidic pH values. For example, endosome lumen exhibits pH values of 4.0–5.5 (Kanamala et al. 2016) and especially lysosomes that exhibit acidic interior pH values of 4.0–5.5, so the response of the nanoparticle to such acidic conditions would be useful towards endosomal escape and targeted drug release (Kanamala et al. 2016; Kim et al. 2016).

The observed pH-responsive ζ-potential can be exploited upon the intracellular accumulation of the nanosystems and the facilitation of endosomal escape. Indeed, the high positive ζ-potential at acidic environment can promote cellular uptake via the negatively charged membranes of the pathological tissues that exhibit lower values of environmental pH. Regarding the endosomal escape after the cell internalization, when the pH of the endosome is lowered from 7.4 to about 5.0, the influx of the H+ and Cl- ions into the endosome is amplified by the absorption of protons by the polyamine. This phenomenon, also called the proton sponge effect, increases the osmotic pressure, leading to disruption of the endosomal membrane and eventually to release of the nanoparticle to cytosol, where the subcellular organelles exist. The cellular internalization and the endosome degradation of the nanoparticles are two major obstacles towards the subcellular organelle delivery. Moreover, the high positive values presented by the nanosystems are ideal for further applications, such as gene delivery, due to the high proton buffering capacity of PDMAEMA (Kanamala et al. 2016; Kim et al. 2016; Lee et al. 2011b).

The presence of serum proteins can strongly affect the physicochemical characteristics of the nanoparticles, because the amphiphilic albumin interacts with the conventional cubosomes being stabilized only by P407 and disintegrates them to small remnant particles, solubilizing the GMO lipid (Leesajakul et al. 2004; Chountoulesi et al. 2018). As it was reported by our previous studies (Chountoulesi et al. 2018, 2019, 2021), the polymeric stabilizer can affect the kind of interaction with serum proteins. For example, the PDMAEMA-b-PLMA and MPOx1 copolymers were found to prevent the albumin-induced disintegration of GMO nanoparticles (Chountoulesi et al. 2019, 2021). When the present liquid crystalline nanoparticles were diluted and incubated in FBS (Table S3), there was an increase of size and size distribution, except from the two systems with PEO-b-PCL copolymers as second stabilizer, where small size decrease of about 10 nm was observed. The observed size decrease is much smaller than the usually observed one for the liquid crystalline nanosystems, in which protein-induced disintegration results to half size values (Chountoulesi et al. 2018, 2019, 2021). The ζ-potential values of all the systems transformed to negative or almost neutral, after their interaction with the negatively charged proteins (Table S3). These physicochemical data indicate that the combination of the polymers utilized resulted in complexation phenomena of the nanoparticles with the serum proteins, preventing disintegration by the albumin. As Azmi et al. 2016 describe, the absence of plasma-induced degradation of liquid crystalline nanostructures is necessary for their successful pharmaceutical application. Thus, the combination of the used polymers can provide promising stealth properties, apart from stimuli-responsiveness and mitochondrial targeting.

The increasing ionic strength affected strongly the ζ-potential, resulting in a gradual decrease of the charge (Figure S5). This phenomenon can be attributed to screening of the positively charged amino groups of the PDMAEMA block (on the TPP groups) by Cl- (NaCl) or phosphate anions (PBS). At 0.34 N, we observed almost total neutralization of the surface in some systems, while three systems were prone to such elevated ionic strengths that presented aggregation phenomena which led to discontinuation of the light scattering measurements. Regarding the other characteristics, there were no significant size alterations in the majority of the cases, while there were some shifts at the size homogeneity (PDI values), probably due to screening effects occurring at the higher ionic strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphological characteristics of the nanosystems as revealed by SLS/DLS and fractal analysis
ce:section.ce:para: From SLS data we calculated the Rg/Rh ratio, which is sensitive to the shape of particles in solution, providing a rough estimation of the morphology of the particle. According to the literature, Rg/Rh ratio assumes the values of 0.775 for a hard uniform sphere, 1.0 for vesicles with thin walls and 1.3–1.5 for random coil (loose) conformation in the case of macromolecular chains (Burchard 1983). When the different systems were dispersed in the pH = 6.0, similar values of Rg/Rh ratio, being close to 0.775, were observed, except for the GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 4:1 nanosystem (Table 1). These values indicate that the studied nanoparticles tend to resemble more to hard uniform spheres, such as the confined liquid crystalline structures, and less to vesicle-like morphologies like liposomes, as it has already been observed for other liquid crystalline nanoparticles (Chountoulesi et al., 2018, 2019, 2021). Moreover, we repeated the SLS measurements in acidic environment with increased temperature as a simulation of the pathological conditions. For example, in the tumor microenvironment there is a distinct hyperthermia in the temperature range of 37 to 42 °C (Zhang et al. 2020). According to the results (Table 1) there were variations of the Rg/Rh ratios with no specific trend, reflecting probably some morphological re-conformation of the nano-assemblies shape (Chountoulesi et al. 2019). Thus, the presence of non-quaternized amine groups on the PDMAEMA chains, able to respond to the environmental changes, seems to affect also the morphological characteristics of the prepared nanosystems in addition to the ζ-potential.

Besides the Rg/Rh ratio calculation, the SLS technique was applied for the estimation of the fractal dimension df , confirming the significant effect of the environmental conditions on the morphology of the systems. In detail, we observed a df increase for all the systems at the pathological conditions, suggesting that the stimuli-responsiveness of the non-quaternized PDMAEMA blocks has been transmitted also to the final nanosystems, as it was confirmed in the case of PDMAEMA-b-PLMA stabilizer (Chountoulesi et al. 2020). We assume that this df increase is mainly caused by the pH decrease (regarding the temperature, it is analytically described in paragraph 3.1.4 that the temperature did not affect the df significantly). According to Chountoulesi et al. 2020, while the pH is decreasing, the amine group of PDMAEMA is becoming more protonated, causing a decrease in the Gaussian curvature and thus a re-arrangement to structures with a lower surface curvature, which is reflected by the increased values of df . The correlation between pH and Gaussian curvature is described also in another recent study (Fong et al. 2020), where the repulsion of the ionized headgroups, due to the pH change, resulted in the decreased surface curvature and consequently to a phase transition from micellar cubic (Fd3m) phase to hexagonal (H2 ) phase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of temperature on the physicochemical and morphological behavior of the nanosystems
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on the physicochemical (size and size distribution) and the morphological characteristics (Rg/Rh ratio and df ) of the prepared nanosystems were investigated at 25 °C, 37 °C (human body temperature), at 55 °C, as well as again at 25 °C after cooling procedure. Regarding their physicochemical characteristics (Fig. 2 a, 2d, Figure S6), the nanosystems exhibited slight alterations during the heating cycle, while some small differences were observed in some systems after the cooling cycle. Similar physicochemical behavior has been reported also in the cases of liquid crystalline nanostructures employing P407, PDMAEMA-b-PLMA, PEO-b-PCL and MPOx copolymers as individual stabilizers (Chountoulesi et al., 2018, 2019, 2021). As in our previous reports (Chountoulesi et al., 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021), the temperature affected more strongly the morphological parameters than the physicochemical ones. In details, in the present systems, there were different trends, mainly non-reversible, of the Rg/Rh ratio vs temperature (Fig. 2b, 2e), reflecting a temperature-induced morphological re-arrangement and disturbance of the shape of the nano-assemblies. The observed alterations of the Rg/Rh ratio of the present systems can be attributed to the PDMAEMA blocks (Chountoulesi et al. 2019), which are thermoresponsive in water solutions, exhibiting LCST in the range 40–50 °C (Plamper et al. 2007; Reis et al. 2010; Mendrek et al. 2014). Above LCST, they become more hydrophobic, due to the destruction of the existing hydrogen bonds between PDMAEMA chains and water, resulting in PDMAEMA chain shrinkage. We should note that PDMAEMA-b-PLMA exhibits a partial irreversibility upon temperature increase was previously reported (Chrysostomou and Pispas, 2018). This consideration could explain the irreversible trend of the Rg/Rh ratio, also observed in this study. Moreover, the GMO nanosystems stabilized individually by PEO-b-PCL or MPOx copolymers present non-reversible alterations of Rg/Rh ratio (Chountoulesi et al. 2021), thereby supporting the obtained results. Nanosystems with PEO-b-PCL or MPOx copolymers as second stabilizer (Fig. 2f) did not exhibit significant differences of df , differently from the systems in which PEO-b-PCL or MPOx copolymers were employed as individual stabilizers (Chountoulesi et al. 2021). Only the GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 4:1 system showed a clear df decrease at 37 °C (Fig. 2c). The observed temperature-induced morphological alterations, in combination with the above described pH-induced morphological alterations (paragraph 3.1.3) and the pH-induced charge inversion (paragraph 3.1.3) can provide further arguments on the stimuli-responsiveness of the prepared nanosystems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphological characteristics of the nanosystems as revealed by Cryo-TEM
ce:section.ce:para: Cryo-TEM results revealed significant morphological differences among the prepared nanosystems, depending on the combination of polymers, as illustrated in Figures 3 and S7, S8. Starting from the GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 9:1 nanosystem (Fig. 3 a), we can observe confined nanoparticles with a highly ordered internal structure and periodicity (Fig. 3a, red arrow). The internal structure of the red box area of this nanoparticle, assessed by the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) (inset Fig. 3a1), gave a space group symmetry likely to Im3m (primitive type bicontinuous cubic phase) (Kuntsche et al. 2011). According to the literature, P407 causes a Pn3m (double-diamond type bicontinuous cubic phase) to Im3m phase transition in GMO-prepared cubosomes (Dong et al. 2006; Gustafsson et al. 1997). Taking into account the case of P407-stabilised GMO systems, where the Im3m space group is caused by the incorporation of the polymeric stabilizer, while the Im3m space group is not observed in the binary phase diagram monoolein/water (Sagalowicz et al. 2006; Zahid et al. 2022), we assume that the TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA copolymer at 10 % w/w% concentration, interacts with the lipid in the same way as P407 does. Secondly, in this system we can observe multilamellar particles of smaller sizes (Fig. 3a, S7 green arrow), with confined, striated, curved inner structure, illustrated by the parallel lines. Generally, the presence of curved striations is considered to be good evidence for the formation of hexosomes, but not a complete proof, because multilamellar or onion-type vesicles can also exhibit curved striations (de Campo et al. 2004; Sagalowicz et al. 2006; Barauskas et al. 2005; Gustafsson et al. 1997; Almgren et al. 1996; Yaghmur et al. 2005; Johnsson et al. 2005). GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 9:1 was the only system exhibiting this type of multilamellar particles with curved striations. Finally, liquid crystalline nanoparticles with intermediate grades of internal organization (Figure S7, yellow arrow), exhibiting less periodical, irregular structures, resembling “sponge”-like structures are also present, along with small sized empty vesicles (Figure S7, blue arrow).

The increase of TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA up to 20 % (i.e., 4:1 ratio) yielded a differentiation of the layout of regular ordered structures. In detail, there was a large population of confined nanoparticles, carrying various kinds of conformations of their crystal lattice (Fig. 3b, red arrows). The internal structure of the red box area of the chosen nanoparticle, assessed by the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis (inset Fig. 3b1), gave a space group symmetry likely to Pn3m (de Campo et al. 2004), highlighting the effect of the polymer concentration on the morphology. The liquid crystalline nanoparticles are co-existing with a prevailing population of small, spherical vesicles with no inner structure (Fig. 3b, blue arrows), as well as sponge-like liquid crystalline nanoparticles with irregular organization (Fig. 3b, yellow arrows). According to previous literature (Gustafsson et al. 1997), the amount of vesicular structures was increased with the increased amount of the stabilizer, such as in the case of P407 stabilizer.

The insertion of the second polymeric stabilizer differentiated further the layout of regular ordered structures. Starting from the block copolymers, in the case of GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:PEO-b-PCL H1 nanosystem, liquid crystalline nanoparticles with a highly ordered inner structure are present (Fig. 3c, red arrow). The internal structure of this nanoparticle, assessed by the FFT analysis (inset Fig. 3c1), gave a space group symmetry likely to Im3m (Kuntsche et al. 2011). A special characteristic of this system, compared to the rest, is that the ordered nanoparticles are covered by intersecting lamellas (Fig. 3c, black arrow). These ''covered'' nanoparticles differ from the characteristic morphology of the sponges (Fig. 3, yellow arrows), which typically consist of an outer layer built of intersecting lamellas and an apparent dense inner core with highly disordered interior and with absence of long-range order and periodicity. The increase of the % wt PCL hydrophobic component in the nanosystem GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:PEO-b-PCL H4 (Fig. S8b) resulted in similar morphological behavior, exhibiting nanoparticles with regularly ordered structures (Fig. S8b, red arrow) attached to vesicles, coexisting with a prevailing population of small, spherical vesicles with no inner structure (Fig. S8b, blue arrow) and sponges (Fig. S8b, yellow arrow). The same types of the objects were present also in the GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:P407 system (Fig. S8a). The internal structure of the nanoparticle, pointed by red arrow, assessed by FFT analysis (inset Figure S8a1 ), gave a space group symmetry likely to Im3m (Kuntsche et al. 2011). We should note that there is an agreement with the previous results (Chountoulesi et al. 2020, 2021), where PEO-b-PCL H1 and P407, being used as individual stabilizers resulted in space group symmetry likely to Im3m.

The insertion of the gradient copolymers MPOx1 and MPOx2 as second polymeric stabilizers yield an increased level of the structures exhibiting higher grades of internal organisation. Starting from the GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:MPOx1 9:1 system, we observed the co-existence of liquid crystalline nanoparticles with both regularly (Fig. 3d, red arrow) and irregularly (Fig. 3d, yellow arrows) ordered structure (sponges), along with small sized empty vesicles (Fig. 3d, blue arrows). The internal structure of the highly ordered nanoparticle (Fig. 3d, red arrow), assessed by the FFT (inset Fig. 3d1), gave space group close to Pn3m, as also was reported in the case of GMO:MPOx1 9:1 nanoparticles (Chountoulesi et al. 2021). The employment of MPOx2 gradient copolymer, which owns higher content of hydrophilic segments, did not differentiate the morphology significantly (Fig. S8c) and led to the same types of nanostructures.

The presence of the dispersed, crowded vesicular structures was common phenomenon in all the systems. We should mention that these vesicles cause a further stabilization of the cubic phase nanoparticles by a coexisting lamellar phase, while the vesicles being attached as interfacial layer to the nanoparticles surrounding them, are actually forming a “shell/coating” layer of material, covering the ordered nanoparticles, and helping to keep them dispersed in aqueous solution (Barauskas et al. 2005; Gustafsson et al. 1997). From a thermodynamic point of view, the formation of a vesicular coating on cubosomes has been suggested as a thermodynamic means of avoiding exposure of lipid hydrocarbon chains to aqueous environment as the cubic liquid crystalline gel is fragmented during dispersion (Spicer and Hayden, 2001).

Taking into account the stimuli-responsive properties of the TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA, the cryo-TEM measurements were repeated for systems, whose pH was decreased to 4.2 (by adding drop-wise hydrochloric acid 1 N) and subsequently were incubated at 42 °C for 30 min, in order to monitor their morphological response in the simulated pathological conditions. As we can observe in Fig. 4 there are obvious differences in the four studied systems, highlighting a clear disruption, comparing with the initial state of the samples that is illustrated in Fig. 3 and was discussed above. More specifically, there is an absence of nanoparticles with regularly ordered structures and periodicity. Only in Fig. 4b, a remaining organized nanostructure (red arrow) was detected, surrounded by attached vesicles. FFT analysis was unable to recognize space symmetries in anyone of the present particles. Spherical small-sized vesicles are coexisting with sponge-like liquid crystalline nanoparticles with disorganized inner structure (Fig. 4, white arrows). We should mention that the present sponges exhibit less grade of internal organization than the typically observed sponge nanoparticles of Fig. 3 (yellow arrows). By observing the interior of the nanoparticles (Fig. 4, black arrows), there are regions with a significant collapse of the structure, with disappearance of their lattice structure, while their intersecting lamellas are extremely tangled forming a disordered interior. Another interesting feature is the presence of some bulgy nanoparticles in different stages of the fusion and destruction processes (Fig. 4, pink arrows). Additionally, in GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 9:1 system there are some kind of structures of great morphological interest (Fig. 4a, S9, light green arrows), that probably are former better organized or multilamellar nanoparticles (Fig. 3a, Figure S9, green arrows) being now disorganized, due to the altered pH and temperature conditions. The present cryo-TEM results illustrate a stimuli-induced morphological re-arrangement and alteration of the nanoassembly shape and are in agreement with the SLS and fractal analysis data described in section 3.1.3. The observed stimuli-responsive morphologies can also be correlated with the drug release results described below.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was applied in order to extract qualitative information regarding the microenvironment of the prepared nanosystems and more specifically the micropolarity (I1/I3 ) and microfluidity (IE/IM ) at two different temperatures (25 °C and 45 °C) and pH values (4.2 and 6.0). For this, pyrene was employed as a hydrophobic probe, being incorporated in the bilayer domains that are composed of the lipid and the hydrophobic blocks of the stabilizers. All results from fluorescence spectroscopy are summarized in Table 2 . Starting from the 25 °C and pH = 6.0 conditions, we can observe that the different polymeric combinations caused differentiation of the micropolarity. The I1/I3 values around 1.04 are close to the ones of the GMO-based nanosystems stabilized by P407, PDMAEMA-b-PLMA, PEO-b-PCL and MPOx copolymers individually (Chountoulesi et al. 2018, 2019, 2021), due to the water swelling occurring in GMO internal nanostructure as a consequence of the stabilizer’s incorporation (Yaghmur et al. 2008). Contrary, I1/I3 values around 0.90 indicate the presence of bilayers of low polarity, such as the phytantriol lipid- based ones, due to a different way of interaction between lipid and polymer (Chountoulesi et al. 2018, 2019). Thereby, not all the different polymeric combinations interacted/co-organized in the same way with the GMO lipid. Regarding the microfluidity at 25 °C and pH = 6.0, the IE/IM values were similar to the GMO-based nanosystems stabilized by PDMAEMA-b-PLMA, PEO-b-PCL and MPOx copolymers individually and lower of the P407-stabilized ones (Chountoulesi et al. 2018, 2019, 2021). Maybe, the strong hydrophobic character of the PLMA and PCL blocks or PhOx segments of the copolymers respectively, being stiffer than the polyether chain of P407, are able to make the bilayer less fluid than P407 in GMO binary systems, allowing a lower incorporation of the hydrophobic pyrene probe.

As long as the 45 °C and pH = 4.2 conditions, there is temperature-induced decrease of micropolarity and increase of microfluidity of all the systems in both pH values. Moreover, at pH = 4.2 there was a slight increase in microfluidity and variations of micropolarity among the systems with no specific trend. While the PDMAEMA stretches out of the bilayer surface, thereby its pH-induced protonation does affect the inner bilayer. However, the temperature induced shrinkage of the PDMAEMA polymeric corona maybe provokes defects at the bilayer, making the membrane more fluid and more permeable to pyrene, and increasing the microfluidity. Taking into account that the PLMA block exhibits high deformability, due to its low glass transition temperature (Tg ca. −53.8 °C) (Demetriou and Krasia-Christoforou, 2008; Chrysostomou and Pispas, 2018), it is less possible to influence the obtained results. A similar trend of the temperature-induced increase of microfluidity has been also reported for the PEO-b-PCL and MPOx copolymers (Chountoulesi et al. 2021). According to the present results, the temperature responsiveness behaviour of the copolymers due to the presence of the polymers, is maintained to the prepared nanosystems, and these properties could be useful for a temperature-induced release of hydrophobic drug cargos.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal characterization of the nanosystems by mDSC and HR-US
Resveratrol entrapment and in vitro release studies
ce:section.ce:para: Τhe ability to entrap the hydrophobic drug resveratrol was investigated and the resultant nanosystems were characterised again in terms of the physico-chemical and morphological properties. Furthermore, it has been evaluated whether they are able to release their content in a stimuli-triggered manner and to respond to the environmental stimuli. As such two different in vitro drug release conditions were tested, namely the physiological one at pH = 7.4 and 37 °C and the pathological one at pH = 4.2 and 42 °C. Resveratrol gets involved to many cellular paths, while it possesses the ability to arrest cell cycle progression, to promote cell differentiation and to induce programmed cell death by caspase-independent or caspase dependent apoptosis or by autophagocytosis (Trincheri et al. 2007). Since resveratrol exhibits various pharmacological activities in a plethora of pathological conditions, which are correlated with subcellular dysfunctions, its targeted delivery would enhance its therapeutic effect.

In all the studied nanosystems resveratrol was incorporated at 2 mg/mL, which is the maximum concentration, providing dispersions without any appearance of drug precipitation. The physicochemical characteristics of the nanoparticles with entrapped resveratrol, as well as the entrapment efficiency (%) are summarised in Table S4. The drug incorporation did not cause significant differences in mean size, compared to the empty respective nanocarriers, but only a small increase in size distributions. Although the ζ-potential showed little variations, it remained at high positive values (> + 25 mV), while the entrapment efficiency (%) was higher than 97.74 % in all the studied systems. In line with the morphological characterisation, obtained by cryo-TEM (Fig. 6 ), we can observe that the resveratrol incorporation did not affect the main characteristic objects present in the empty nanosystems (Fig. 3). Specifically, there are liquid crystalline nanoparticles with the regularly ordered inner structure (Fig. 6, red arrows), co-existing with sponge liquid crystalline nanoparticles with intermediate grades of internal organization (Fig. 6, yellow arrows) and vesicles with no inner structures (Fig. 6, blue arrows). Unfortunately, all the observed high-ordered nanoparticles were too small in size, in order to carry out a FFT analysis. Moreover, an interesting aspect about the GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:MPOx1:RSV 8:1:1 system is the formation of cyclic nanostructures (Figure S10, black arrows), whose membrane thickness resembles polymersome-like structures (Gumz et al. 2019).

Regarding the release studies (Fig. 7
), all the nanosystems at pH = 7.4 and 37 °C, exhibited a sustained resveratrol release until 4–8 h, depending on the systems, after that a plateau was observed, regardless of the conditions. A significant observation is that there were significant differences for all the nanosystems between the two sets of conditions. Statistical analysis was performed by using Student’s t-test and multiple comparisons were done using one-way ANOVA. P-values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant. The obtained release profiles (Fig. 7) at 37 °C and pH = 7.4 are similar to those reported for other hydrophobic drugs from liquid crystalline nanoparticles, in which the plateau at non-sink conditions was reached at drug concentrations lower than 100 % release (Boyd et al. 2006; Thorn et al. 2020).

More specifically, at pH = 7.4 and 37 °C, the nanosystems presented an initial drug release (10–20 % of the total amount of resveratrol) during the first hour that can be attributed to the large surface area of the colloidal dispersions (Mansour et al. 2017), followed by a prolonged release after the first hour that can be related to the retention of the drug inside the liquid crystalline nanoparticles structures (Badie and Abbas, 2018; Elgindy et al. 2016). This prolonged release yielded maximum cumulative mass resveratrol release percentages between 20 and 40 % depending on the polymeric stabilizer utilized. Taking into account the lipophilicity of resveratrol, and the high values calculated for the EE%, we assume that the drug resides in the GMO bilayer.

Different maximum cumulative mass resveratrol release level (%) was observed for each system, which indicates that the different polymeric stabilizer has a remarkable effect on the release profile. This can be attributed to the fact that the different polymeric stabilizers can determine changes in the nano-tubular aqueous network and different ratios of each morphology in each nanosystem, as was depicted by the morphological studies. For example, the GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:MPOx1:RSV nanosystem exhibited less percentage of vesicles (structures with no tortuous network of channels) and higher content of organized structures (Fig. 6d), which can contribute to the lowest observed cumulative mass resveratrol release (%). The more organized nanostructures, like the cubic ones, lead to a more sustained release, because drug has to diffuse through highly tortuous porous morphologies (Mansour et al. 2017; Kulkarni et al. 2017).

Contrariwise, at pH = 4.2 and 42 °C, GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 9:1 and 4:1 nanosystems presented an initial drug release (up to 30 %) during the first three hours, while the other two systems containing two stabilizers exhibited a burst drug release up to 50 % within the first three hours. In all the systems, there was a following sustained release that resulted to maximum cumulative mass resveratrol release levels that were higher than the respective ones in physiological conditions. The systems containing the two stabilizers (Fig. 7b) presented even more than doubled level of RSV release at pH = 4.2 and 42 °C in comparison to physiological conditions (pH 7.4, 37 °C). The obtained release patterns at pH = 4.2 and 42 °C in all the systems confirm the stimuli-responsive behaviour of the nanosystems. The morphological re-arrangements to disorganized, bulgy structures, observed by cryo-TEM analysis (Fig. 4), in response to pH and temperature changes, are also reflected by the higher drug release levels at such conditions. We assume that these stimuli-induced re-arrangements can promote drug release. The observed triggered cargo release at acidic pH values of the nanosystems would be very useful for the triggered delivery of drugs in the acidic interior of the lysosomes.

Moreover, the release kinetics of resveratrol from the nanoparticles were analyzed by fitting the in vitro release data into mathematical models based on zero-order, first-order and Higuchi kinetic equations and then computing their correlation coefficients (R2) (Badie and Abbas, 2018). According to the results, the release was found to fit better to the Higuchi model, thus being diffusion controlled in all the studied cases, in both physiological and pathological simulated conditions, with R2 ranging from 0.844 to 0.961. The results are in agreement with previous studies (Badie and Abbas, 2018; Mansour et al. 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro toxicity and confocal microscopy in cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: Dose-dependent cell viability studies were performed on normal (non-cancerous) fibroblast-like cell line COS-7 treated with 1, 5, 10 and 50 μΜ of GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA 9:1 and 4:1, GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:MPOx1 8:1:1 and GMO:TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA:PEO-b-PCL H1 8:1:1 dispersions. The obtained results (Figure S11) show that the nanosystems have no statistically significant different cell viability in comparison to the control sample, even at the highest tested concentration of 50 μΜ, indicating the absence of cytotoxicity, at least for the studied concentrations.

Moreover, to probe the sub-cellular localization of the nanoparticles, we used CLSM analyses conducted on nanoparticles labeled with Rhodamine-PE lipid. From the obtained images (Fig. 8) it is clear that nanoparticle red fluorescence is partially co-localized with LysoTracker Green (Pearson's correlation coefficient 0.35), revealing that the nanoparticles are located primarily in lysosomes. From the images, it is also clear that the nanoparticle fluorescence is also observed in spherical structures that resemble lysosomes (Stagni et al. 2020). We assume that the high polycationic content of the nanosystems, due to the delocalized TPP cations, can promote an efficient cellular internalization of the nanoparticles, while the stimuli-responsive ability can facilitate the endosomal escape and therefore their delivery to subcellular organelles as lysosomes.

We assume that the lysosomal localization of the nanosystems is promoted by the higher content of free amino groups in the PDMAEMA block than the quaternized ones, thereby lysosomal localization prevailed over the rest subcellular organelles, due to the acidotropic effect. For example, Liu et al. (2020) developednovel carbon dots that could specifically be distributed in lysosomes and this phenomenon was attributed to the large amount of the amino groups of the nanosystems. Since the nanosystems with amino groups entered into cells through the endocytosis process, the acidotropic effect of the amino group helps them to largely accumulate in the acidic lysosomes (Liu et al., 2019; Shuang et al. 2018).

As it has been already reported, the targeting ability of the present nanosystems to the lysosomes is considered to be of great therapeutic significance, because lysosomes are considered to be also a therapeutically important organelle of various lysosomal dysfunction related diseases, such as lysosomal storage disorders, cancer and neurodegenerative disorders, such as Alzheimer’s Disease and autoimmune diseases (Wang et al., 2018; Pinto e Vairo et al. 2020; Bonam et al. 2019; Platt et al. 2012; Del Grosso; Papadimitriou et al. 2022; Yin et al. 2022). Targeting lysosomes can trigger apoptotic and lysosomal cell death pathways, as well as it can play an important role in overcoming cancer drug resistance by sequestering cancer drugs in their acidic environment. In the case of lysosomal disorder diseases, where lysosomal function is impaired, enzyme replacement therapy provides lysosomal enzymes that are lacking or are absent in the patients (Del Grosso et al. 2022). Towards this strategy, the utilization of lysosome targeting nanovehicles would probably improve precise lysosomal delivery. For example, Álvarez et al. (2019) developed nanostructured lipid carriers for the delivery of elosulfase alpha to lysosomes, as a potential therapy for treatment of lysosomal storage disease of mucopolysaccharidosis type IVA (Morquio A syndrome).

Non-lamellar liquid crystalline nanosystems were prepared from GMO lipid and stabilized by the multi-functional amphiphilic block copolymer TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA. The stabilizer is composed of the stimuli-responsive PDMAEMA blocks that were partially quaternized by the delocalized cation TPP. The above polymer was used either alone or in combination with other polymeric stabilizers, namely Poloxamer P407, PEO-b-PCL block copolymers or MPOx gradient copolymers that all exhibit high biocompatibility, low toxicity, as well as promising stealth properties. The TPP-QPDMAEMA-b-PLMA either alone or in combination with the other polymers resulted in GMO-based nanoparticles colloidally stable upon time. According to the physicochemical characterization, all the prepared nanosystems exhibited high positive values of ζ-potential, due to the high polycationic content of the TPP groups, and to the free amino groups of the non-quaternized PDMAEMA blocks. The systems also exhibited a pH-induced ζ-potential conversion to increased positive values in acidic conditions and decreased positive values at a physiological pH. This property can be exploited for the intracellular accumulation of the nanosystems and the complexation with subcellular organelles. Furthermore, the combinations of the polymers utilized prevented the disintegration of the nanoparticles by serum proteins, yielding a possible increase of stealthiness. The fractal dimension, as well as the Rg/Rh ratio, obtained by SLS data indicated pH- and thermo- induced morphological/structural rearrangements that were also confirmed by cryo-TEM results.

MTT assay assessed absence of cytotoxicity of the nanosystems, while CLSM studies in rhodamine-labeled nanoparticles revealed localization to the subcellular organelles of acidic lysosomes, probably due to their stimuli-responsive profile and the presence of high percentage of free amino groups.

The observed stimuli-responsive physicochemical and morphological behavior were reflected in increased resveratrol release levels at pH and temperature values simulating the pathological conditions and comparing with the physiological ones, as well as simulating the microenvironmental conditions of lysosomes. Since resveratrol exhibits various pharmacological activities in a plethora of pathological conditions, which are correlated with subcellular dysfunctions, its targeted delivery along with enhanced release in acidic conditions like those in lysosomes would enhance its therapeutic effect.

In conclusion, to the best of the authors knowledge, lyotropic liquid crystalline formulations with multi-functional properties were developed for the first time, showing dual stimuli-responsive (pH- and thermo-) physicochemical and morphological behavior, a stimuli-triggered drug release, stealthiness, as well as subcellular targeting ability to the lysosomes.
